metadata.title: Writing Hookify Rules
metadata.topic: WRITING HOOKIFY RULES · CLAUDE CODE SKILL

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to create a hookify rule, write a hook rule, configure hookify, or add a hookify rule — the Writing Hookify Rules skill fires. A hookify rule is a markdown file with YAML frontmatter that defines patterns to watch for and messages to show when those patterns match. Rules live in dot-claude slash hookify dot rule-name dot local dot md files, and are read dynamically on every tool use. Five event types: bash watches commands, file watches edits, stop watches completion, prompt watches user input, all watches everything. Let me walk you through the anatomy, the event system, and where this skill gets it right and where it bites.

props.topic: WRITING HOOKIFY RULES · CLAUDE CODE SKILL
props.command: Create a hookify rule that warns when I try to use rm -rf.
output: 5 events: bash · file · stop · prompt · all — simple or condition-based
Pattern: Python regex · action: warn (default) or block

beats.act: anatomy — rule file format + frontmatter fields
beats.narration_text: A hookify rule has two parts: a YAML frontmatter block and a markdown message body. The frontmatter has four required fields and one optional. Name: required, kebab-case, verb-first — warn-dangerous-rm, block-console-log. Enabled: boolean, true activates, false disables — toggle without deleting. Event: which hook fires — bash for command patterns, file for Edit and Write operations, stop for completion checks, prompt for user input. Pattern: Python regex to match against — rm backslash s plus -rf for dangerous rm commands, console backslash dot log backslash open-paren for debug output. The action field is optional and defaults to warn, which shows the message and allows the operation; set it to block to prevent the operation on PreToolUse hooks or stop the session on Stop events. The markdown body after the frontmatter is the message shown to Claude when the rule triggers. Good messages explain what was detected, why it is problematic, and what to do instead. Rules are read dynamically: create or edit the file, test immediately on the next tool use.

props.sparkLine: Name it. Event it. Pattern it. Message it. One markdown file, immediate effect.

beats.narration_text: Four event types. Bash events match Bash tool command strings — rm backslash s plus -rf catches rm -rf, sudo backslash s plus catches privilege escalation. File events match Edit, Write, and MultiEdit operations — pattern matches new_text by default, but the advanced format lets you match on file_path, old_text, or content instead. Stop events match when the agent wants to stop — a catch-all pattern dot-star with a checklist body enforces completion steps. Prompt events match user input content. The simple format uses a single pattern field. The advanced format uses a conditions array — each condition specifies field, operator, and pattern, and all conditions must match for the rule to trigger. Six operators: regex_match, contains, equals, not_contains, starts_with, ends_with. Common pitfalls: too-broad patterns — pattern log matches catalog and login, not just console dot log. Too-specific — rm -rf /tmp only catches one path. And YAML escaping: quoted strings require double backslashes, so use unquoted patterns.

props.sparkLine: bash catches commands. file catches edits. stop catches completion. conditions stack.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. Writing Hookify Rules gets the format right: file location and naming are explicit and unambiguous — dot-claude slash hookify dot name dot local dot md, with the hookify prefix, the dot-local suffix, the dot-md extension. The two-tier rule design — simple pattern versus advanced conditions — is clearly distinguished with complete examples for each. The event type guide is concrete: each of the four event types has a working example showing what it matches and how to write the pattern. Pattern writing tips are practical: escaping rules, metacharacters, a one-liner test command, and three common pitfalls with bad and good examples. The message body guidance — explain what, explain why, suggest alternatives — is actionable. Where it bites: the block action is described but never demonstrated — what does the user or Claude actually see when a rule blocks an operation? The conditions system for stop and prompt events does not document what fields are available — for stop events is there a stop_reason field? For prompt events is user_prompt the only option? No documentation of rule execution order or whether multiple rules can trigger on the same tool call. And the all event type is listed but has no example and no performance or ordering note.

props.sparkLine: Format solid. Block action and stop/prompt condition fields: document them.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Writing Hookify Rules fires for creating hookify rules, writing hook rules, configuring hookify. Format: dot-claude slash hookify dot name dot local dot md — frontmatter name plus enabled plus event plus pattern or conditions, plus optional action warn or block. Five events: bash commands, file edits, stop completion, prompt user input, all. Conditions: field plus operator plus pattern, all must match. Body: what detected, why problematic, alternatives. Gaps: block action undemo'd; stop and prompt condition fields undocumented; rule ordering not documented; all event no example.

artifactLines: 5 events: bash · file · stop · prompt · all
Simple: pattern field · Advanced: conditions[] (field + operator + pattern, ALL match)
action: warn (default, allows) or block (prevents PreToolUse / stops session)
Body: what detected · why problematic · suggest alternatives
Gaps: block undemo'd · stop/prompt fields unknown · ordering undefined · all: no example

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session in any project. Paste this: Create a hookify rule that blocks rm -rf commands, and one that warns when editing .env files. Watch four things. First: does the rm -rf rule use event bash with pattern rm backslash s plus -rf and action block? The dangerous-command rule needs block, not warn — warn still lets the command run. Second: does the .env rule use a conditions array with file_path matching backslash dot env dollar, rather than just a new_text pattern that would miss the file path? Third: are both files named in dot-claude slash hookify dot something dot local dot md format with the hookify prefix and dot-local dot md suffix? Fourth: do both message bodies explain what was detected, why it matters, and what to do instead — not just a one-line warning? If the rm rule uses action warn instead of block — it allows the command through, which is the opposite of what was asked. That is your gate.

props.command: Create a hookify rule that blocks rm -rf commands, and one that warns when editing .env files.
props.runningText: Watch: event type correct? pattern right? action block vs warn? naming convention?
output: Expected: bash event + rm\s+-rf + action: block for rm rule
Expected: file event + conditions file_path: \.env$ for env rule + action: warn
Red flag: warn on rm rule · missing .local.md suffix · no alternatives in body

beats.narration_text: Writing Hookify Rules. Liam, in for Bear.
